cause: cold impl-judge round 1 returned IMPLEMENTATION_PASS false on a real would-have-shipped bug: a capture never checked for duplicate labels among the region's panes, so a region with two panes sharing a title produced a template carrying two identical labels — which the schema rejects outright, because a label is the apply manifest's key. The capture therefore wrote a file that failed its own validator, silently and with no warning. Live-reproduced by the judge against real tmux, and again independently before the fix.
fix: captureLayout now collects the region's duplicated labels up front and drops each from EVERY pane carrying it, pushing one warning through the existing warnings channel — the same degrade-and-warn shape the outside-the-root dir case already uses. Dropping both rather than keeping the first is deliberate: nothing in the region says which pane the author meant by a shared name, so picking one would invent an answer. One additive scenario was appended to the frozen suite to specify the behavior rather than leave it as an unnamed implementation choice; additive, so the freeze self-clears (gherkin-cli diff: addOnly true). Fixed in code only — the frozen contract was never edited to fit the implementation. Fix verified live against a real herdr region with two panes both labeled worker: warns, drops both, and the saved template validates.

cause: the test bound to the frozen scenario 'a captured template passes validate' was inert — its title claimed the capture validates, but its body only asserted the name and description came back, so it would have passed for a template carrying a cwd, a duplicate label, or a degenerate ratio. It is the scenario that should have caught the duplicate-label bug above and could not.
fix: the test now runs the real validateLayout over the captured template and asserts zero errors, over a richer fixture (a label, a nested dir, and a pane outside), plus the negative case that a mismatched filename stem fails. Found while fixing the duplicate-label blocker, not reported by either judge.

cause: cold impl-judge round 2 returned IMPLEMENTATION_PASS false: three new frozen scenarios were bound to tests that never exercised the CLI-observable behavior their Then clauses name. Two name a warning written to stderr and that the template is still written; one names an exit code and that nothing is written. All three were bound to pure-module tests asserting only captureLayout's returned warnings array, or only that it throws. The judge proved the gap was real rather than pedantic by mutation: disabling the stderr-forwarding line in cli.ts left the whole suite green, so a regression silently dropping every layout save warning would have shipped undetected. Partly self-inflicted — a CLI-level warning test existed and was deleted earlier in this mission as a supposed duplicate of the unit test; it was not a duplicate, it was the only check of the stderr half of the contract.
fix: the scenario-bound test for each of the three moved to CLI level (cli.test.ts, under the spec:cyber-mux/layout block), asserting stderr content, the written file, the exit code and write-suppression as the Then clauses name; the pure-module tests were kept as unit coverage of the derivation under titles that no longer claim the whole scenario, so the bridge binds the test that actually checks the whole Then. Re-ran the judge's own mutation to confirm the fix is not theatre: disabling stderr forwarding now fails 2 tests where it previously passed 97/97. Test-only change; no edit to layout-capture.ts, cli.ts, or the frozen contract.

cause: cold impl-judge round 3 returned IMPLEMENTATION_PASS false on two more over-claiming checks, both found by mutation rather than reading. First: the check bound to the draft-note scenario asserted only the first of its Then's two clauses, so removing 'add a command to each pane' from the note left it green. Second, and subtler: the check bound to the label scenario used a fixture with TWO panes carrying tmux's hostname default, so a broken host-title filter would label both with the hostname, making them duplicates — which this CR's own new duplicate-label drop then removed, producing byte-identical output. The scenario passed for entirely the wrong reason, and a regression in the host filter would have shipped green. The CR's own earlier fix was what masked it.
fix: the draft-note check now asserts both clauses. Both label fixtures (the unit one and the CLI one) now carry exactly ONE host-titled pane, which takes the duplicate-label path out of the picture so the check fails if and only if the host filter itself breaks. Re-ran both of the judge's mutations to confirm: breaking the host filter now fails 2 tests, and dropping the note's second clause fails 1, where both previously passed green. Test-only; no production code or frozen contract touched. Caught a process defect while doing it — the first fix attempt threw before its write and silently persisted nothing, so the mutation re-run rather than the edit's apparent success is what proved it landed.
